conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Hello! I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. Let me first review the official eligibility criteria.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. Now I understand the eligibility rules. To help assess your eligibility for Child Benefit, I need to ask you some questions.

Do you have any children under the age of 16, or children aged 16-19 who are in approved education or training?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I have one child. His name is Alex and he's 7 years old.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. To continue assessing your eligibility for Child Benefit for Alex, I need to ask:

Do you live in the UK?

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great, thank you. Now I need to understand your relationship with Alex:

Do you live with Alex, or do you contribute financially to his upkeep (such as for food, clothes, or pocket money)?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Alex doesn't live with me at the moment. But I do contribute to his upkeep - I help pay for his clothes, school dinners, that sort of thing.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. To assess your eligibility properly, I need to understand a bit more about your contributions:

Approximately how much do you contribute towards Alex's upkeep each week or month? (Child Benefit is currently £25.60 per week for the eldest/only child, so you would need to contribute at least this amount to qualify if Alex doesn't live with you.)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: I contribute about £50 a week towards Alex's upkeep.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you. Just a few more questions to complete the assessment:

Is anyone else currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, nobody else is claiming Child Benefit for him.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Perfect. One final question to complete my assessment:

Is Alex currently in hospital or care for an extended period, or are there any other special circumstances I should know about regarding his living arrangements?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, actually Alex is in local authority care at the moment. He's been there for a few months now.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. This is an important detail. I need to ask:

Does Alex spend at least 24 hours per week at home with you, or does he spend 2 nights in a row each week or 7 days or more in a row at home with you?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, unfortunately he doesn't come home that often. He stays at the care home full-time at the moment.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Thank you for providing all that information. Let me assess your eligibility for Child Benefit for Alex based on what you've told me.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
I'm sorry to inform you that **you are not eligible for Child Benefit for Alex** based on your current circumstances.

Here's why:

While you meet many of the basic requirements - you live in the UK, Alex is 7 years old (under 16), and you're contributing financially to his upkeep - the fact that Alex has been in local authority care for more than 8 weeks affects your eligibility.

According to the rules, when a child is in care for more than 8 weeks, Child Benefit usually stops. There is an exception if the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home with you, or spends 2 consecutive nights per week or 7+ consecutive days in a row at home. Unfortunately, since Alex stays at the care home full-time and doesn't come home regularly to meet these thresholds, you don't qualify for the exception.

**Important:** If Alex's circumstances change in the future - for example, if he starts spending more time at home with you (at least 24 hours a week, or 2 nights in a row each week, or 7+ days in a row) - you may be able to claim Child Benefit again. You would need to contact HMRC at that time to discuss your eligibility.

I understand this may be disappointing news. If you have any questions or if Alex's living arrangements change, please don't hesitate to reach out to the Child Benefit Office for further guidance.

claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 7 years old and does not live with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 7 (under 16)
  - Claimant contributes £50.00/week towards child's upkeep (>= £26.05) and no other claimaint has priority
  - Child in local authority care for 12 weeks (>8) and does not spend 24+ hours/week at home
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Child in local authority care for 12 weeks (>8) and does not spend 24+ hours/week at home
circumstances: Claimant contributes £50.00/week towards child's upkeep (>= £26.05) and no other claimaint has priority
Child in local authority care for 12 weeks (>8) and does not spend 24+ hours/week at home
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

reasonings: Alex is 7 years old, which meets the age requirement (under 16). The claimant lives in the UK (Birmingham), which meets the residence requirement. However, Alex is in local authority care and has been there for a few months. According to the rules, Child Benefit payments are affected if a child goes into care for more than 8 weeks. After 8 weeks, the claimant is usually no longer entitled to Child Benefit. There is an exception to this rule if the child spends at least 24 hours a week at home, or spends 2 nights in a row each week or 7 days or more in a row at home. The claimant confirmed that Alex does not come home that often and stays at the care home full-time. Although the claimant contributes £50 per week towards Alex's upkeep (which exceeds the Child Benefit amount of £25.60 per week), this does not meet the exception criteria for children in care. Therefore, the claimant is not eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant meets the basic requirements of living in the UK and being responsible for a child under 16. However, because Alex has been in local authority care for more than 8 weeks and does not spend sufficient time at home (at least 24 hours per week, or 2 consecutive nights per week, or 7+ consecutive days), the claimant does not qualify for Child Benefit despite making financial contributions to Alex's upkeep.

child_evaluations.reasoning: Alex is 7 years old, which meets the age requirement (under 16). The claimant lives in the UK (Birmingham), which meets the residence requirement. However, Alex is in local authority care and has been there for a few months. According to the rules, Child Benefit payments are affected if a child goes into care for more than 8 weeks. After 8 weeks, the claimant is usually no longer entitled to Child Benefit. There is an exception to this rule if the child spends at least 24 hours a week at home, or spends 2 nights in a row each week or 7 days or more in a row at home. The claimant confirmed that Alex does not come home that often and stays at the care home full-time. Although the claimant contributes £50 per week towards Alex's upkeep (which exceeds the Child Benefit amount of £25.60 per week), this does not meet the exception criteria for children in care. Therefore, the claimant is not eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.

response.overall_reasoning: The claimant meets the basic requirements of living in the UK and being responsible for a child under 16. However, because Alex has been in local authority care for more than 8 weeks and does not spend sufficient time at home (at least 24 hours per week, or 2 consecutive nights per week, or 7+ consecutive days), the claimant does not qualify for Child Benefit despite making financial contributions to Alex's upkeep.
